title: Component Preparation and Precleaning
narrationScript: Magnetic particle testing has a reputation for being simple. Magnetize the part, apply the particles, read the indications. And it is simple, right up until the surface is wrong. Then the method quietly stops working, and it will not tell you it stopped. The particles are tiny, the leakage fields that hold them are weak, and anything that sits between the field and the particles, or anything that keeps the particles from moving freely, costs you sensitivity you may never know you lost. That is why every written procedure starts the same way. Prepare the part. Clean the surface. Dry it. Only then magnetize. Start with component preparation, which is more than wiping the part down. Before the part ever reaches the bench, look at what it is and what is attached to it. Fittings, covers, and hardware that block the surfaces you need to examine come off. Openings that should not take in particles or bath get plugged or masked. Think about an engine crankshaft with drilled oil passages. Run it through a wet bath with those passages open and you fill them with suspension and iron oxide particles, and you may send abrasive debris straight into a rebuilt engine's bearings. Bearings on any component get protected for the same reason, and as a rule we avoid magnetizing them at all, because a residual field collects wear debris in service. If the part arrives carrying a strong residual field from earlier handling, it may hold particles in meaningless patterns, so it may need demagnetizing before you start. And if you plan a head shot or prods, the electrical contact areas deserve special attention. Current has to flow into the part through those points, and paint, scale, rust, or oil at a contact raises resistance. The result is arcing, and an arc strike is not cosmetic. It is a tiny weld with its own heat affected zone, and on hardened material it can crack, which means you just created the kind of defect you were hired to find. Contact areas must be clean bare metal, with firm contact made before the current goes on and held until it is off. Now precleaning itself. The goal is a surface that is clean and dry, free of oil, grease, rust, loose scale, sand, paint, and anything else that interferes, over the area of interest plus about an inch beyond it on all sides. There are three families of methods, and you choose based on what the contaminant is and what the part can tolerate. Solvent cleaning handles oil and grease. That can be a hand wipe with solvent on a lint free cloth, a soak tank, or vapor degreasing, where hot solvent vapor condenses on the part and rinses it continuously. Solvents are fast and gentle to the metal, but they only remove what dissolves. They will not touch rust or scale. And the part must dry completely, because solvent left in a crack can keep particles or bath from entering it, and solvent carried into a wet bath degrades the suspension. Chemical cleaning covers alkaline cleaners and acids. Hot alkaline solutions strip heavy grease, shop soils, and some carbon deposits. Acid pickling dissolves rust and tight scale that solvents ignore. Chemical methods reach where brushes cannot, but they come with rules. Acid on high strength steel can drive hydrogen into the metal, and that hydrogen can crack the part later in service. That is hydrogen embrittlement, and procedures that allow acid cleaning of susceptible parts typically require a bake afterward to drive the hydrogen back out. Everything chemical also ends with a thorough rinse and complete drying, because residues attack particle baths and skew results. Mechanical cleaning is the muscle. Wire brushing, abrasive blasting, grinding, and tumbling remove rust, heavy scale, old paint, sand, and weld spatter. But mechanical methods cut both ways. Aggressive blasting, peening, or hard wire brushing on softer material can smear a thin layer of metal across the surface and close the very cracks you are looking for. Blasting can also pack a crack full of debris so neither bath nor powder can enter it. So you use the gentlest mechanical method that does the job, and on critical soft surfaced parts the procedure may call for a light etch afterward to reopen smeared metal. One more habit separates professionals. Check the surface again after cleaning, just before you magnetize. Is it dry. Is it cool enough for the particles you plan to use. Are the contact areas bare. The minutes you spend preparing the part are not the chore before the inspection. They are the first step of the inspection, and the validity of everything that follows rides on them.
imagePrompt: A technician in safety glasses and nitrile gloves wipes oily residue from a steel crankshaft journal with a solvent-dampened lint-free cloth at a workbench beside a wet horizontal magnetic particle bench unit in an industrial inspection shop.

sections.heading: Why Surface Condition Decides the Test
sections.body:
Magnetic particle testing depends on a chain of weak signals. A discontinuity disturbs the flux, a small leakage field forms at the surface, and finely divided particles migrate into that field and hold there. Every link in that chain can be broken by a dirty surface.

- **Oil and grease** glue particles wherever they land, kill the mobility that lets them migrate to a weak leakage field, keep dry powder from dusting evenly, and contaminate a wet bath the moment the part enters it.
- **Rust, loose scale, and sand** hold particles mechanically, generate false indications, and shed debris into the bath.
- **Paint and other coatings** lift the particles away from the surface, where the leakage field is weakest — their effect on sensitivity is the subject of the next lesson.
- **Moisture** clumps dry powder and ruins an oil-carrier suspension.

The required end state is simple to state and absolute in practice: the examination surface must be **clean and dry** before particles are applied. Typical procedures expect the cleaned and examined surface to extend about **one inch beyond** the specific area of interest on all sides, so flaws at the edge of the zone are still judged on a valid surface.

sections.heading: Component Preparation Before Cleaning
sections.body:
Preparation starts before any cleaner touches the part.

- **Disassemble and expose.** Remove covers, fittings, and hardware that hide surfaces you are required to examine.
- **Plug and mask openings.** Drilled oil passages, galleries, bearing bores, and threaded holes are plugged or masked so particles and bath cannot enter. A crankshaft run through a wet bench with open oil passages collects particle-laden suspension that later bleeds back out as false drainage patterns — and can carry abrasive residue straight into a rebuilt engine's bearings.
- **Protect what magnetism can damage.** Bearings and races are kept out of the field where possible; a residual field attracts wear debris in service.
- **Check residual magnetism.** A part arriving with a strong residual field from prior handling can hold particles in meaningless patterns (magnetic writing) and may need demagnetization before the examination starts.
- **Prepare electrical contact areas.** For a head shot or prods, the current enters through the contact points — they must be bare, clean metal under firm pressure.
- **Confirm temperature.** Hot parts limit your options: typical procedures cap wet suspensions around **135 °F**, while some dry powders are usable to roughly **600 °F**.

sections.title: Arc strikes: damage you create
sections.body: With a head shot or prods, **never make or break contact while current is flowing**, and never energize on a dirty contact point. Paint, scale, or oil at a contact raises resistance, and the result is an **arc strike** — a tiny weld with its own heat-affected zone that can crack hardened material. Arc strikes are rejectable damage under many specifications, and the risk is one reason a number of aerospace practices prohibit prods on finished surfaces outright. Dirty contacts also make current delivery unreliable, so the field you actually get can be weaker than the amperage setting implies.

sections.heading: Solvent Precleaning
sections.body:
Solvent cleaning removes what dissolves: oils, greases, and many cutting and preserving fluids.

- **Hand wipe** with solvent on clean, lint-free cloths — the everyday field method for local examinations.
- **Tank soak or spray** for batches of small parts.
- **Vapor degreasing** — the part hangs in hot solvent vapor that condenses and continuously rinses it; fast and consistent for production work.

Know the limits. Solvent does **not** remove rust, scale, sand, or paint. The surface must then **dry completely**: solvent trapped in a discontinuity can keep particles or bath from entering it, and solvent dragged into a wet bath degrades the suspension. Use solvents with adequate ventilation, away from ignition sources, and per the safety data sheet — and on hot parts, check the part temperature against the solvent's flash point before you wipe.

sections.heading: Chemical Precleaning
sections.body:
Chemical methods reach contamination that solvents ignore.

- **Alkaline cleaning** — hot alkaline solutions, by soak or spray, strip heavy grease, shop soils, and some carbon deposits. Parts must be rinsed thoroughly and dried; alkaline residue contaminates baths and can attack the surface.
- **Acid pickling** — dilute acids dissolve rust and tightly adherent scale. A mild acid etch is also the standard remedy for reopening a crack mouth that mechanical work has smeared shut.
- **Paint strippers** — chemical removers take coatings down to bare metal where the procedure requires it.

The controlling caution is **hydrogen embrittlement**. Acid cleaning generates atomic hydrogen at the steel surface; high-strength steels absorb it and can crack in a delayed fashion after returning to service. Procedures that permit acid cleaning of susceptible parts typically pair it with a **bake-out** at a specified temperature and time. Every chemical method ends the same way: complete rinse, complete dry, and a check that no residue remains to interfere with the particles or the bath.

sections.heading: Mechanical Precleaning
sections.body:
Mechanical methods remove what chemistry cannot lift: loose and heavy scale, sand, weld spatter, and old coatings.

- **Wire brushing and hand abrasives** for local cleanup along welds.
- **Abrasive blasting** for large areas of scale and failed paint.
- **Grinding** where the surface itself must be dressed — for example, weld ripple that would trap particles and mask interpretation.
- **Tumbling** for batches of small castings and forgings.

Mechanical cleaning is also where preparation can quietly destroy the examination. Aggressive blasting, peening, hard wire brushing, or coarse grinding on relatively soft material can **smear a thin layer of metal across the surface and close the mouth of a fine crack**, hiding it from any surface method. Blasting can likewise **pack a crack with debris** so neither bath nor powder enters it. Use the least aggressive method that does the job, keep grinding cool so you do not add grinding cracks of your own, and on critical soft-surface parts expect the procedure to require a light **etch** after mechanical work to restore detectability.

sections.caption: Matching the precleaning method to the contaminant — typical selections and their cautions.
rows: Solvent wipe, soak, or vapor degrease; hot alkaline soak
Dry completely; ventilation and flash point; will not remove rust or scale
Hydrogen embrittlement on high-strength steel — bake out; blasting can smear or pack cracks
Use the gentlest method that works; smearing risk on soft metals
Bare metal required at electrical contact points before head shots or prods
Surface must be fully dry before particles are applied

sections.caption: A clean, dry surface lets particles migrate to the leakage field and bridge the crack; an oil film or soil layer holds them away and immobile, and the indication dies.
Precleaning selection chart — match the method to the contaminant, then verify the surface is clean, dry, and undamaged before magnetizing.

checkQuestions.question: A wet fluorescent examination is planned on a part still filmed with light machine oil. What is the most fundamental reason the oil must come off first?
options: Oil increases the part's magnetic permeability and distorts the field
Oil holds particles where they land, blocks their migration to weak leakage fields, and contaminates the bath
Oil prevents the part from reaching magnetic saturation
Oil only matters for dry powder, so the wet bath could be used as-is
checkQuestions.explanation: Indications form because mobile particles drift to weak leakage fields and hold there. An oil film destroys that mobility and masks the field — and one oily part starts degrading the suspension for every test that follows.

checkQuestions.question: Before a crankshaft with drilled oil passages goes onto a wet horizontal bench unit, the passages should be:
options: Flushed with bath suspension so they are pre-wetted
Left open so the suspension can clean them out
Plugged or masked so particles and bath cannot enter
Enlarged slightly to improve drainage
checkQuestions.explanation: Open passages collect particle-laden bath that later bleeds out as false drainage patterns and can carry abrasive residue into the bearings when the engine is assembled. Plugging and masking is standard component preparation.

checkQuestions.question: A head shot is set for 1,200 A, but the contact plates will bear on heavy mill scale at both ends of the part. If it is run as-is, the technician should expect:
options: Nothing unusual — the machine setting guarantees the same current regardless of contact condition
Arcing and burning at the contact points, with unreliable current delivery into the part
A stronger circular field, because scale concentrates the current
Only a cosmetic problem, since arc strikes polish out
checkQuestions.explanation: Scale makes a high-resistance contact: it invites arc strikes — small welds with heat-affected zones that can crack hardened material — and it makes the current actually flowing through the part unreliable. Contact areas must be bare, clean metal under firm pressure.

checkQuestions.question: Acid pickling is proposed to remove rust from a high-strength steel landing-gear component before MT. The special concern with this choice is:
options: The acid will demagnetize the part
Hydrogen embrittlement — absorbed hydrogen can cause delayed cracking, so a controlled bake-out is typically required afterward
Acid permanently raises the part's coercive force
Acid leaves a ferromagnetic residue that causes false indications
checkQuestions.explanation: Acids generate atomic hydrogen at the steel surface; high-strength steels absorb it and can crack later in service. Procedures that permit acid cleaning of susceptible parts pair it with a specified bake to drive the hydrogen back out.

checkQuestions.question: After aggressive abrasive blasting of a soft low-carbon steel weldment, suspected fine toe cracks no longer show on MT. The most likely explanation is:
options: Blasting relieved the residual stresses, closing the cracks permanently
Blasting work-hardened the steel so cracks can no longer form
Smeared metal has closed the crack mouths and debris may be packed into them, so a light etch may be needed to restore detectability
Blasting demagnetized the part
checkQuestions.explanation: Peening action on soft material smears surface metal over fine crack openings and can pack the cracks with debris. The flaws are still there but sealed off from the leakage-field mechanism; an etch reopens the surface.

references: CP-105-2024 MT Topical Outline §3.2 (precleaning: solvent, chemical, mechanical)
ASTM E709 and ASTM E1444/E1444M (general practice: surface condition, contact areas, examination coverage)
ASME BPVC Section V, Article 7 (general practice: surface preparation)
